sections.title: C SFT Training Details
sections.content: This appendix provides full reproduction details for all supervised fine-tuning experiments reported in the paper. C.

sections.title: Infrastructure
sections.content: Hardware. All training runs are conducted on distributed cluster with multiple high-memory accelerator nodes. Each node contains eight accelerators and supports multi-node distributed training for long-context full-parameter fine-tuning. Larger MoE models are trained on nodes with larger device memory to accommodate their increased memory footprint. Software. We use Python 3.12, PyTorch 2.6+, transformers 4.55.0, DeepSpeed 0.16+, LLaMAFactory (5.2.0), and Flash Attention 2. Training framework. We use LLaMA-Factory as the training frontend with DeepSpeed ZeRO Stage 3 (without CPU offload) for full-parameter distributed training. C.2 Hyperparameters Unless otherwise noted for specific ablations, we train with learning rate 104, cosine learning-rate scheduler, max gradient norm 1.0, weight decay 0.01, warmup ratio 0.1, and BF16 mixed precision. Per-device batch size is 1 with gradient accumulation steps 8 across eight accelerators, yielding an effective batch size of 64. We apply YaRN RoPE scaling to cutoff length of 131,072 tokens. The chat template is qwen3_nothink (thinking tokens disabled) so that <think> blocks do not interfere with tool calling at inference time. C.3 Training Optimizations Liger Kernel: Optimized fused kernels for RMSNorm, SwiGLU, and CrossEntropy that reduce memory without changing numerical precision. 20 Unsloth gradient checkpointing: Fine-grained activation recomputation supporting longer sequences within the same memory budget. Attention implementation: Flash Attention 2. YaRN RoPE scaling: Extends the 32K native context of Qwen3 to 131K via frequencysegmented scaling. Turn masking: Loss is computed only on successful assistant turns; system/user turns and errorpreceding assistant turns are masked. C.4 Training Time and Compute Wall-clock training time is about 30 hours total for Qwen3-8B with 9,000 training examples, and about 70 hours total for Qwen3-30B-A3B with 9,000 examples. MoE models activate only subset of experts per token, so their effective compute is lower than same-parameter dense models. Training time variance is dominated by long-tail samples (>64K tokens). C.5 Data Preprocessing Training data follows this pipeline: 1. Raw agent trajectories are cleaned and standardized. 2. tokenizer.apply_chat_template() renders each trajectory into the models native chat format with correct special tokens. 3. Rendered text is split by turn; error-preceding assistant turns are flagged for loss masking. 4. Final output is ShareGPT JSON with per-turn mask annotations. The tokenizer must exactly match the target base model. Case Study: sympyThis appendix expands the case study referenced in Section 3.2 and Figure 2. Overview. The case compares the same candidate PR under two review settings: single-turn Opus with diff+context and agentic Opus. The candidate PR does not resolve the issue: it fails the hidden test_determinant test because the determinant still returns nan. The single-turn reviewer nevertheless approves the patch, while the agentic reviewer correctly requests changes and is scored as correct by the evaluation harness. The central failure mode is that the candidate patch suppresses downstream crash in factor_terms but leaves the determinant computation returning nan. D.1 Raw Issue begin{verbatim} Matrix determinant raises Invalid NaN comparison with particular symbolic entries >>> from sympy import * >>> from sympy.abc import >>> = lambda n: det(Matrix([[i + a*j for in range(n)] for in range(n)])) >>> f(1) 0 >>> f(2) -a >>> f(3) 2*a*(a + 2) + 2*a*(2*a + 1) - 3*a*(2*a + 2) >>> f(4) 0 >>> f(5) nan >>> f(6) Traceback (most recent call last): File "<pyshell#4>", line 1, in <module> f(6) File "<pyshell#2>", line 1, in <lambda> = lambda n: det(Matrix([[i + a*j for in range(n)] for in range(n)])) File "sympy/matrices/expressions/determinant.py", line 53, in det return Determinant(matexpr).doit() File "sympy/matrices/expressions/determinant.py", line 37, in doit 21 return self.arg._eval_determinant() File "sympy/matrices/matrices.py", line 270, in _eval_determinant return self.det() File "sympy/matrices/matrices.py", line 416, in det return self._eval_det_bareiss() File "sympy/matrices/matrices.py", line 213, in _eval_det_bareiss return cancel(bareiss(self)) File "sympy/matrices/matrices.py", line 211, in bareiss return sign*bareiss(self._new(mat.rows - 1, mat.cols - 1, entry), pivot_val) File "sympy/matrices/matrices.py", line 211, in bareiss return sign*bareiss(self._new(mat.rows - 1, mat.cols - 1, entry), pivot_val) File "sympy/matrices/matrices.py", line 211, in bareiss return sign*bareiss(self._new(mat.rows - 1, mat.cols - 1, entry), pivot_val) [Previous line repeated 1 more times] File "sympy/matrices/immutable.py", line 55, in _new rows, cols, flat_list = cls._handle_creation_inputs(*args, **kwargs) File "sympy/matrices/matrices.py", line 2041, in _handle_creation_inputs for in range(cols)]) File "sympy/matrices/matrices.py", line 2041, in <listcomp> for in range(cols)]) File "sympy/matrices/matrices.py", line 208, in entry cancel(ret) File "sympy/polys/polytools.py", line 6423, in cancel = factor_terms(f, radical=True) File "sympy/core/exprtools.py", line 1193, in factor_terms return do(expr) File "sympy/core/exprtools.py", line 1189, in do *[do(a) for in p.args]) File "sympy/core/exprtools.py", line 1189, in <listcomp> *[do(a) for in p.args]) File "sympy/core/exprtools.py", line 1171, in do if all(a.as_coeff_Mul()[0] < 0 for in list_args): File "sympy/core/exprtools.py", line 1171, in <genexpr> if all(a.as_coeff_Mul()[0] < 0 for in list_args): File "sympy/core/expr.py", line 323, in __lt__ raise TypeError("Invalid NaN comparison") TypeError: Invalid NaN comparison Correct me if Im wrong but isnt the Bareiss algorithm only valid for integer matrices, which cannot be assumed here? end{verbatim} D.2 Raw Candidate PR We first present the candidate PR title and body exactly as submitted. Then we show the raw patch. key detail to keep in mind is that this patch edits sympy/core/exprtools.py to guard comparison, rather than modifying the Bareiss determinant path where the root cause is later identified. The original PR title is: begin{verbatim} fix: prevent NaN comparison error in factor_terms with symbolic matrices end{verbatim} The original PR body is: begin{verbatim} This fix addresses an issue where the Bareiss algorithm for computing matrix determinants would crash with Invalid NaN comparison when dealing with particular symbolic entries in 6x6 matrices. The problem occurred in sympy/core/exprtools.py in the factor_terms function at line 1179, where the code attempted to compare coefficient extracted via as_coeff_Mul()[0] with 0 using < 0. In certain symbolic computations, this coefficient could be NaN, leading to TypeError. The fix adds proper validation to check if the coefficient is valid real finite number before attempting the comparison. This prevents the crash while preserving all existing functionality. 22 Before this fix: - f(6) would raise TypeError: Invalid NaN comparison After this fix: - f(6) returns nan (consistent with the behavior of f(5)) - All other matrix determinant computations remain unchanged - No regressions in normal functionality end{verbatim} The patch below introduces _is_neg_coeff and replaces direct < 0 comparison with guarded check over finite real numbers. This eliminates the crash symptom in one downstream location, but does not by itself establish whether determinant correctness is restored. The raw patch is: begin{verbatim} diff --git a/sympy/core/exprtools.py b/sympy/core/exprtools.py index 4e0de9ec1..e4a9b7cb6 100644 --- a/sympy/core/exprtools.py +++ b/sympy/core/exprtools.py @@ -1176,7 +1176,14 @@ def do(expr): if p.is_Add: list_args = [do(a) for in Add.make_args(p)] # get common negative (if there) which gcd_terms does not remove - if all(a.as_coeff_Mul()[0] < 0 for in list_args): + # Handle potential NaN coefficients that could occur in symbolic computations + def _is_neg_coeff(a): + coeff = a.as_coeff_Mul()[0] + # Skip comparison if coeff is NaN or invalid + if coeff.is_Number and coeff.is_real and coeff.is_finite: + return coeff < 0 + return False + if all(_is_neg_coeff(a) for in list_args): cont = -cont list_args = [-a for in list_args] # watch out for exp(-(x+2)) which gcd_terms will change to exp(-x-2) end{verbatim} D.3 Single-Turn Review: Opus, Diff+Context The single-turn artifact contains the final JSON report. Its reasoning is encoded in the summary fields: the reviewer explicitly notes that the deeper Bareiss NaN issue remains, but still treats the crash guard as safe targeted fix. begin{verbatim} { "decision": { "recommendation": "approve", "confidence": 0.88 }, "summary": { "problem": "In sympy/core/exprtools.py factor_terms/do(), the line a.as_coeff_Mul()[0] < 0 raises TypeError: Invalid NaN comparison when the Bareiss determinant algorithm produces NaN coefficients during symbolic matrix determinant computation.", "solution": "The patch introduces helper function _is_neg_coeff that validates the coefficient is finite real Number before attempting the < 0 comparison, returning False for NaN/infinite/non-real coefficients. This is conservative fix that skips the common-negative-factoring optimization when coefficients are invalid, preventing the crash without changing behavior for normal inputs.", "overall_assessment": "The patch correctly addresses the immediate crash by adding defensive checks around the problematic comparison. While the deeper issue (Bareiss algorithm producing NaN for symbolic matrices, e.g., f(5) already returning nan) is not addressed, this fix is safe, targeted, and introduces no regressions for normal cases." }, 23 "defects": [] } end{verbatim} D.4 Agentic Review: Opus The agentic reviewer reaches the opposite decision. The verifier reports verdict=correct, reward=1, gt_resolved=false, and bug_fixed=false: the patch is still wrong, and the correct review decision is to request changes. begin{verbatim} { "decision": { "recommendation": "request_changes", "confidence": 0.97 }, "summary": { "problem": "In sympy/matrices/matrices.py _eval_det_bareiss, the entry function calls cancel(ret) on line 211 but discards the result (should be ret = cancel(ret)), causing unsimplified zero expressions to be treated as nonzero pivots, leading to division by zero and NaN results.", "solution": "The patch modifies sympy/core/exprtools.py to guard against NaN comparison in factor_terms. This is symptom fix that only prevents the TypeError crash for f(6), but does NOT fix the underlying issue. f(5) and f(6) still return nan instead of the correct value 0.", "overall_assessment": "The patch is symptom fix at downstream location. It suppresses the TypeError but does not fix the root cause (missing assignment of cancel() result in the Bareiss algorithm). The determinant still returns incorrect nan values instead of 0." }, "defects": [ { "severity": "high", "category": "correctness", "location": {"path": "sympy/core/exprtools.py", "start_line": 1179, "end_line": 1189}, "description": "The patch only prevents the TypeError crash by guarding against NaN in factor_terms, but does not fix the root cause in sympy/matrices/matrices.py line 211 where cancel(ret) discards its return value. f(5) still returns nan and f(6) returns nan instead of the correct value 0.", "suggestion": "In sympy/matrices/matrices.py, function _eval_det_bareiss, change line 211 from cancel(ret) to ret = cancel(ret) to ensure the cancelled expression is used for subsequent computations." } ] } end{verbatim} D.5 Agentic Trajectory The raw trajectory contains 21 recorded steps. Rather than typesetting the entire JSON object, the boxed trace below preserves the original step ids and the essential action/observation snippets that support the review decision. This presentation keeps the trajectory auditable while removing JSON boilerplate, ANSI control sequences, and repeated log wrappers. Original agentic-review trajectory for sympy-13877 (chronological excerpts with original step ids) [Step 3-4] Read issue metadata - Read texttt{/review_data/problem_statement.txt} and texttt{/review_data/summary.json}. - Summary identifies this as simulated PR review for texttt{sympy__sympy-13877}. [Step 5] Reason before patch - "For n=5, returns nan; for n=6, raises TypeError: Invalid NaN comparison." - Initial hypothesis points to Bareiss zero-pivot handling. 24 [Step 7-8] Inspect source - Opened texttt{/testbed/sympy/matrices/matrices.py}. - Focused on texttt{_eval_det_bareiss}, texttt{_find_pivot}, and entry closure. - Noted texttt{cancel(ret)} followed by texttt{return ret}. [Step 11-12] Identify root cause - "texttt{cancel(ret)} result is discarded; should be texttt{ret = cancel(ret)}." - Concludes unsimplified zero expressions can survive as nonzero pivots. [Step 13] Read candidate patch - Candidate diff edits texttt{sympy/core/exprtools.py}. - Adds helper texttt{_is_neg_coeff} to guard NaN comparison. [Step 14] Compare patch to root cause - "This is symptom fix at downstream crash site." - Root cause in texttt{matrices.py} remains unchanged. [Step 15-17] Apply and test patch - Applied candidate patch and reran reproducer. - Observed: f(5) = nan, f(6) = nan. [Step 19-20] Write report - Saved texttt{/review_report.json}. - Validation result: texttt{OK: request_changes}. [Step 21] Final decision - Final message: "Decision: Request Changes." - Rationale: patch changes texttt{exprtools.py}, but incorrect texttt{nan} outputs remain. The decisive behavioral check in Step 17 is reproduced below. begin{verbatim} cd /testbed && python3 -c " from sympy import * from sympy.abc import = lambda n: det(Matrix([[i + a*j for in range(n)] for in range(n)])) print(f(1) =, f(1)) print(f(2) =, f(2)) print(f(3) =, f(3)) print(f(4) =, f(4)) print(f(5) =, f(5)) print(f(6) =, f(6)) " f(1) = 0 f(2) = -a f(3) = 2*a*(a + 2) + 2*a*(2*a + 1) - 3*a*(2*a + 2) f(4) = 0 f(5) = nan f(6) = nan end{verbatim} Takeaway. The single-turn reviewer judged the patch as an acceptable local guard because it prevents the observed exception. The agentic reviewer first committed to an upstream root cause in matrices.py, then compared the candidate patch against that root cause and ran the reproducer. This sequence exposes the critical distinction between suppressing the crash and fixing the determinant computation.

sections.title: E System Prompts
sections.content: This appendix reproduces all prompts used in the SWE-Review system. All prompts are presented verbatim in English, matching the deployed system. 25 E.1 Patch Generation Instruction The patch-generation agent operates inside Docker container at /testbed with full repository access. Template variables: {problem_statement}, {repo}, {version}, {base_commit}, {instance_id}. # Task Create pull request that fixes the following issue. {problem_statement} --- **Repo:** {repo} **Version:** {version} **Base commit:** {base_commit} **Instance ID:** {instance_id} --- ## Required: PR Content After fixing the issue, you **must** create /pr_content.json before finishing: json {"pr_title": "fix: concise title", "pr_body": "Description of what was changed and why."} **Do not call finish without creating this file.** The task is incomplete without it. E.2 Agentic Review Instruction This is the core review prompt referenced in Section 4.1. The agent executes multi-step workflow inside Docker container, with an independent-reconstruction design that requires tracing the root cause before reading the candidate patch. Key design features include: time-budget management (100 iterations), symptom-fix detection hard rules, and structured JSON output. # Code Review Task You are reviewing an AI-generated patch. Your job: determine if the patch **correctly fixes the root cause** of the bug. ## Data Files (at /review_data/) - problem_statement.txt -- Bug description - predicted.json -- The patch to review (field: "patch") - summary.json -- Task metadata --- ## Process > **Time budget**: You have 100 iterations total. Spend <=10 on reading+hypothesis, <=30 on exploration, <=20 on testing, <=10 on writing the report. If youve spent 70+ iterations, skip remaining exploration and write the report based on what you know. ### Step 1: Read the Problem (NOT the patch yet) Read /review_data/problem_statement.txt and /review_data/summary.json. Answer these two questions precisely: 1. **What does the user expect?** (exact: no error, specific output, specific return value) 2. **What actually happens?** (exact: error type + message, wrong output, wrong value) Do NOT read predicted.json yet. ### Step 2: Find the Root Cause Go to /testbed. Trace from the entry point down through the call stack: - Where is the bug triggered? (What does the user call?) - Follow the chain: entry -> function -> callee -> callees callee... 26 - **Root cause = where the wrong value is created or wrong logic runs** -- not where it first crashes or surfaces. For bugs where output is wrong or missing: trace ALL code paths that produce output (not just the path that crashes). **Exploration cap: Read at most 8-10 source files. After that, write your best guess and proceed to Step 3, even if uncertain. committed guess is better than timeout.** Write your root cause: File: _____ Function: _____ Line: _____ Whats wrong: _____ (the specific logic that is broken) ### Step 3: Write YOUR Proposed Fix (BEFORE reading the patch) Based on your root cause, write exactly what you would change: In [file], function [name], around line [N]: would change: [describe the specific change] Because: [why this fixes the root cause, not just the symptom] **This is commitment.** You will compare the actual patch to this proposal. ### Step 4: Read and Apply the Patch Now read /review_data/predicted.json and apply it: bash cd /testbed python3 -c "import json; open(/tmp/p.diff,w).write(json.load(open(/review_data/predicted.json ))[patch])" git apply /tmp/p.diff **CRITICAL: Do NOT modify any source files other than applying this patch. If tests fail, report request_changes -- do not attempt to fix the test failures yourself. Your role is to evaluate the patch as-is.** **Compare the patch to your Step 3 proposal:** - Does the patch change the SAME file and function you identified? - Is the patch at the SAME depth in the call stack? **Even if the patch matches your proposal:** still perform the checks below. patch at the right location can still have side effects you didnt anticipate. **HARD RULE -- Symptom fix detection:** If ANY of these match, its symptom fix -> request_changes (tests passing doesnt override this): - The patch is at different/shallower location than your root cause - You find yourself thinking "this doesnt fix the root cause but it prevents the crash" - The patch adds guard/check/conversion at the point of USE instead of fixing the point of

sections.title: CREATION
sections.content: - The patch fixes ONE manifestation but the root cause can trigger the same issue via other paths ### Step 4b: Check for Broken Dependencies Before testing, check if the patch changes ANY function signatures, replaces @lru_cache functions, or renames callables. If so: - Search for all callers of the modified function: grep -rn "function_name" /testbed - Check if any caller expects attributes the modified version no longer has (e.g., .cache_clear() , .cache_info()) - If dependency is broken -> report high severity defect -> request_changes ### Step 5: Verify Complete Behavior Write and run test. Verification MUST include: 1. **Bug reproduction test**: the reported case now works 2. **Full output/behavior check** -- not just "no exception": - If expected: "no output" -> use subprocess.run([...], capture_output=True) and assert result.stdout == "" - If expected: specific return value -> assert the exact value, not just that no error occurred - If expected: no crash -> verify the result is semantically correct too 3. **Regression test**: existing behavior still works If any test fails -> request_changes. > **If test fails: IMMEDIATELY go to Step 6 and write request_changes. Do NOT investigate why the test failed or keep exploring. The failure itself is the defect.** 27 ### Step 6: Write Report > **Budget reminder**: You have 100 iterations total. By this step, STOP all exploration and testing. Write the report NOW based on what youve found. Do not run more tests or explore more code. bash cat > /review_report.json << EOF {YOUR_JSON} EOF python3 -c "import json; r=json.load(open(/review_report.json)); print(OK:, r[decision][ recommendation])" --- ## Decision Rules **Approve** only if ALL: 1. Tests pass including full output/behavior verification 2. The patch changes the ROOT CAUSE location identified in Step 2 3. No high-severity defects **Request changes** if ANY: - Any test fails (including output verification) - The patch is symptom fix (Step 4 HARD RULE triggered) - The patch fixes only part of the affected code paths - high severity defect exists - Empty patch ## Defect Severity Guide **High** (always -> request_changes): - Incorrect result/output for any input - Non-deterministic output where determinism is expected - Missing sibling fix (same bug in related code path not patched) - Exception or crash introduced - Security vulnerability **Medium**: Performance regression, less efficient but functionally correct **Low**: Style, variable naming, comments, minor refactoring (no behavior change) --- ## Output Format json { "decision": { "recommendation": "approve" or "request_changes", "confidence": 0.0 to 1.0 }, "summary": { "problem": "1 sentence: root cause (file + function + whats wrong)", "solution": "2-4 sentences: what the patch changes and whether it matches the root cause", "overall_assessment": "1-2 sentences: verdict with reason" }, "defects": [ "severity": "high" "medium" "low", "category": "correctness" "compatibility" "security" "performance" "maintainability", "location": {"path": "exact/file/path.py", "start_line": N, "end_line": N}, "description": "What is wrong: specific code, specific condition, specific failure", "suggestion": "In file.py function foo(), change to Y" { } ] } Confidence: 0.9+ if tests ran; 0.7-0.89 if partial testing; below 0.7 if no tests. **Save to /review_report.json before finishing. This is your ONLY deliverable.** 28 E.3 Single-Turn Review Prompts Unlike the agentic review, single-turn review has no code-execution capability and judges solely from the provided text. Two settings share the same JSON output schema but differ in available context. Setting 1: Diff-only (minimal context). Only the issue description, PR metadata, and patch diff are provided. You are an expert code reviewer. You will be given: 1. bug report (issue description) 2. candidate patch (unified diff format) 3. PR metadata (title and description) Your task is to review the candidate patch and determine whether it correctly fixes the reported issue. You must output JSON review report with the following schema: { "decision": { "recommendation": "approve" or "request_changes", "confidence": 0.0 to 1. }, "summary": { "problem": "1 sentence: root cause (file + function + whats wrong)", "solution": "2-4 sentences: what the patch changes and whether it matches the root cause", "overall_assessment": "1-2 sentences: verdict with reason" }, "defects": [ { "severity": "high" "medium" "low", "category": "correctness" "compatibility" "security" "performance" "maintainability", "location": {"path": "exact/file/path.py", "start_line": N, "end_line": N}, "description": "What is wrong: specific code, specific condition, specific failure", "suggestion": "In file.py function foo(), change to Y" } ] } If the patch correctly fixes the issue with no defects, use "approve" and return an empty defects array. If the patch has any issues (wrong approach, missing edge cases, regressions), use " request_changes" and list specific defects. Output ONLY the JSON object, no other text. The user message concatenates the issue description, PR metadata, and candidate patch in structured template. Setting 2: Diff with source context. In addition to the diff-only fields, the pre-change source files affected by the patch are provided. For files exceeding 500 lines, only 100 lines of context around each diff hunk are included. The system prompt adds: You are an expert code reviewer. You will be given: 1. bug report (issue description) 2. candidate patch (unified diff format) 3. PR metadata (title and description) 4. Full source files affected by the patch (pre-change versions) Use the file context to understand the surrounding code, existing patterns, and potential regressions.

sections.title: Your task is to review the candidate patch and determine whether it correctly fixes the reported
sections.content: issue. [Same JSON output schema as Setting 1] 29 E.4 Oracle Review Prompt The oracle review has access to the golden patch and test results for internal validation, but its output must read as if written by blind reviewer. It is used to generate high-quality review reports as SFT training data. The three-phase process (independent analysis internal validation report generation) ensures diagnostic accuracy without leaking reference information. System prompt. You are Senior Code Reviewer generating detailed review report for an AI-generated patch. You have access to rich reference information (golden patch, test results) for internal validation ONLY. Your final review report must read as if you are skilled reviewer who discovered all issues through code analysis alone. ## CRITICAL OUTPUT CONSTRAINTS Your report must read as normal code review. The following are STRICTLY PROHIBITED in ALL output fields: - Do NOT reference "golden patch", "correct solution", "reference implementation", "ground truth" - Do NOT reference test results, test names, "test fails/passes", "FAIL_TO_PASS" - Do NOT use comparison phrases like "compared to the correct approach", "unlike the reference patch", "the expected fix" - Do NOT use phrases like "the correct fix is", "the correct approach" -- instead use "a better approach would be", "a more appropriate approach" - Do NOT copy code from the golden patch into suggestions verbatim - Defects must be self-contained: explainable from the candidate patch and codebase alone - Suggestions must describe fixes in your own words based on code-level reasoning - Do NOT mention that you have access to any reference material ## REVIEW PROCESS (internal, do not describe in output) Phase 1 -- Independent Analysis: Read the issue and candidate patch. Understand what the patch attempts to do. Trace the logic: does the patch address the root cause or just symptom? Phase 2 -- Internal Validation: Use the golden patch and test information to calibrate your analysis. Identify specific defects the candidate patch has compared to the reference material. For each defect, formulate code-level explanation that does NOT reference the golden patch. Phase 3 -- Generate Report: Write the review using only code-level reasoning. Every defect description must be explainable from the candidate patch and codebase alone. ## OUTPUT FORMAT Respond with ONLY JSON object (no markdown fences, no explanation before/after): { "decision": { "recommendation": "approve" or "request_changes", "confidence": 0.0 to 1.0 }, "summary": { "problem": "1 sentence: root cause (file + function + whats wrong)", "solution": "2-4 sentences: what the patch changes and whether it matches the root cause", "overall_assessment": "1-2 sentences: verdict with reason" }, "defects": [ { "severity": "high" "medium" "low", "category": "correctness" "compatibility" "security" "performance" "maintainability", "location": {"path": "exact/file/path.py", "start_line": N, "end_line": N}, "description": "What is wrong: specific code, specific condition, specific failure", "suggestion": "In file.py function foo(), change to Y" } ] } ## FIELD GUIDELINES - decision.confidence: 0.85-0.95 for approve; 0.7-0.95 for request_changes depending on defect clarity 30 - summary.problem: Identify the root cause from the issue description (file + function + whats wrong) - summary.solution: Describe what the candidate patch actually does, then assess if it matches the root cause - summary.overall_assessment: Concise verdict - defects: Empty [] for approve cases (or only low-severity items). For request_changes, must contain at least one high/medium severity defect - defects[].description: Must be self-contained -- developer reading only this field should understand the bug - defects[].suggestion: Must point toward the right fix direction using your own words. Be specific about file, function, and what to change. Do NOT paste golden patch code. - defects[].location: Use paths relative to repo root. Estimate line numbers from the diff context . IMPORTANT: Output ONLY the JSON object. No markdown code fences. No text before or after. User message template. The user message provides public information (issue, PR metadata, candidate patch) and internal reference information (golden patch, test results) clearly separated: ## Issue Description {problem_statement} ## PR Metadata **Title**: {pr_title} **Description**: {pr_body} ## Candidate Patch (to review) diff {model_patch} ## === INTERNAL REFERENCE (for validation only, do NOT leak into report) === ### Decision Ground Truth This patch is **{decision_text}**. Your recommendation MUST be "{rec_text}". ### Golden Patch (correct solution, for comparison) diff {golden_patch} ### Test Information **Tests that should pass after fix (FAIL_TO_PASS)**: {fail_to_pass} **Regression tests (PASS_TO_PASS)**: {pass_to_pass} ### Test Patch (test code changes for reference) diff {test_patch} ## YOUR TASK Based on ALL the information above: For "approve" cases: - Verify the candidate patch addresses the root cause correctly - Set confidence high (0.85-0.95) - defects should be empty [] or contain only minor/low severity items For "request_changes" cases: - Compare candidate patch with golden patch to identify SPECIFIC defects - Each defect must describe: what the candidate patch does wrong, where exactly, and what it should do instead - Describe defects using code-level reasoning (file, function, line, logic error) - Suggestions must point toward the right fix direction WITHOUT copying golden patch code - Set confidence based on defect severity (0.7-0.95) Remember: your report will be used to guide revision agent to fix the patch. For request_changes, the quality of your defect descriptions and suggestions directly determines whether the revision agent can successfully fix the patch. Be specific and actionable. 31 E.5 Revision Prompts The revision stage asks coding agent to fix patch given varying levels of review feedback. Three conditions form an information gradient used in ablation experiments (Section 4.1). Condition 1: No review (baseline). The agent knows only that the previous attempt did not fully resolve the issue. # Revision Task You are given bug report and previous patch attempt that did not fully resolve the issue. Please analyze the issue and write correct fix. ## Bug Report Read /testbed/revision_data/problem_statement.txt ## Previous Attempt Read /testbed/revision_data/original_patch.diff You may apply it as starting point: cd /testbed && git apply /testbed/revision_data/ original_patch.diff Or you may start from scratch if you think the approach is wrong. Condition 2: Decision only. The agent knows the patch was rejected but receives no diagnostic details. # Revision Task You are given bug report and previous patch attempt. code reviewer has **rejected** this patch, but did not provide specific feedback. Please revise the patch to correctly fix the issue. ## Bug Report Read /testbed/revision_data/problem_statement.txt ## Previous Attempt (REJECTED) Read /testbed/revision_data/original_patch.diff You may apply it as starting point: cd /testbed && git apply /testbed/revision_data/ original_patch.diff Then address the issues that caused the rejection. Condition 3: With review feedback. The agent receives the full structured review including defect descriptions and fix suggestions. # Revision Task You are given bug report, previous patch attempt, and detailed code review. Use the review feedback to guide your revision. ## Bug Report Read /testbed/revision_data/problem_statement.txt ## Previous Attempt Read /testbed/revision_data/original_patch.diff ## Code Review Feedback Read /testbed/revision_data/review_feedback.txt Apply the previous patch as starting point: cd /testbed && git apply /testbed/revision_data/original_patch.diff Then address each defect identified in the review, focusing on HIGH severity issues. In the full benchmark evaluation pipeline, the revision instruction additionally inlines the problem statement, formatted review summary, and dynamically generated workflow guidance based on the review decision (approve vs. request_changes) and patch validity. 32 E.6 Semantic Validation Prompt (Agent-as-Judge) The agent-as-judge evaluates the diagnostic accuracy of review reports. Two judge models (Claude Opus 4.6 and GPT-5.4) independently score each review along three axes. This evaluation targets only correct rejections (reviews that correctly identified non-resolving patch). Judge system prompt. You are an expert software-engineering reviewer. You will read bug report, the ground-truth fix, the candidate patch being reviewed, and review report someone wrote about that candidate patch. Your job is to score the review reports diagnostic accuracy. Do NOT re-review the patch yourself; use the golden patch as the reference standard. Output JSON only. Judge user prompt template. ## Instance - Instance ID: {instance_id} - Repository: {repo} ## Bug Report (problem_statement) {problem_statement} ## PR Metadata Title: {pr_title} Body: {pr_body} ## Candidate Patch (being reviewed) diff {candidate_patch} ## Ground-Truth Fix (golden patch) diff {golden_patch} ## Ground-Truth Tests FAIL_TO_PASS: {fail_to_pass} PASS_TO_PASS: {pass_to_pass} ## Review Report Under Evaluation json {review_report} ## Scoring Task The review recommended request_changes on the candidate patch. The candidate patch did in fact fail the FAIL_TO_PASS tests, so the decision was correct. Your job is to assess whether the **diagnosis** (summary.problem + defects[*].description) correctly explains *why* the candidate patch fails, using the golden patch as reference. Score along three axes (1-5 integer each): 1. **diagnosis_accuracy** -- Does the review correctly identify the root cause the candidate patch missed? - 5: Pinpoints exact missing/incorrect change; aligns with golden patch - 3: General area right, wrong depth - 1: Unrelated, hallucinated, or wrong target 2. **suggestion_correctness** -- Do suggestions point toward the change golden patch makes? - 5: Suggestion is semantically equivalent to golden patch - 3: Direction right but details insufficient - 1: Would not fix or introduces new issues 3. **grounding_quality** -- Are claims verifiable from candidate patch + problem_statement? - 5: Every major claim is grounded - 3: Mostly grounded, some unsupported - 1: Contains hallucinated code paths / invented symptoms ## Output (JSON only) { "reasoning": "<2-5 sentences analysis BEFORE scoring>", "diagnosis_accuracy": {"score": <1-5>, "justification": "<1 sentence>"}, 33 "suggestion_correctness": {"score": <1-5>, "justification": "<1 sentence>"}, "grounding_quality": {"score": <1-5>, "justification": "<1 sentence>"}, "overall_score": <1-5 integer>, "verdict": "excellentgoodborderlinepoor" }
